input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue with keyboard handling in .NET 10. This is a known change — in .NET 9+, `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was deprecated/removed because Android itself deprecated `adjustResize`. The recommended approach changed.

Let me provide a concise answer based on my knowledge of .NET MAUI's evolution.

In .NET MAUI for Android, the `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` approach relied on Android's `adjustResize` soft input mode, which was deprecated in Android 11 (API 30). 

For .NET 10 (and even late .NET 9), the recommended approach is to use the `Edge-to-edge` / `WindowInsets` API approach, or more specifically in MAUI, you should use the `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` behavior or handle it via the new `IKeyboardAutoScroll` interface.


The old `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` configuration method was replaced starting in .NET 8 with `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll`, which handles keyboard appearance automatically. By .NET 9/10, the underlying `adjustResize` mode became obsolete, so the modern solution relies on edge-to-edge display with proper inset handling instead. The core issue is that `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` handles scrollable content automatically, but for a fixed chat input bar at the bottom, you need to manually listen to keyboard insets and adjust padding accordingly. I'll walk through the three main approaches: relying on the built-in auto-manager, handling `WindowInsetsCompat` directly on Android, or falling back to `adjustPan` mode if needed.
